topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on the screen, stark white against the dark background of my laptop. "For Sale: 14 Willow Creek Lane." My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that did little to fill my lungs. I stared at the listing, the familiar crooked mailbox at the end of the drive suddenly feeling like a ticking time bomb. My hands began to tremble so violently I had to grip the desk edge to steady them. *No. This isn't real. It has to be a mistake.* I furiously clicked the link to the real estate site, my eyes darting back and forth between the photos—the peeling paint, the overgrown garden—and the listing details. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, squeezing the air from my chest. I needed to call someone, anyone, but my throat was suddenly dry, refusing to form a coherent sentence. Every instinct screamed at me to run, to bolt from the screen, but my feet felt rooted to the floor.

[story 2]

I reread the address three times, my reflection swimming in the darkened monitor. Fourteen Willow Creek Lane. The name echoed in the silence of my small apartment, a sound that felt heavy and suffocating. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim. I dropped the mouse, the small clatter sounding deafening in the quiet room. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drum solo demanding attention. I scrambled for my phone, my fingers clumsy and slick with sudden sweat. I dialed my mother, my voice coming out thin and reedy, a stark contrast to the roaring chaos inside my head. "Mom? Have you seen anything about Willow Creek?" I whispered, waiting for a response that felt impossibly far away. The silence on the line stretched, thick with unspoken dread.

[story 3]

The image of the old porch swing, rusted and swaying in the imagined breeze, filled my mind. A dizzying sense of dislocation seized me. I leaned back in my chair, pushing away from the desk as if the screen itself were radiating heat. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my temples. This was impossible. I cycled through every possible explanation—a prank, a typo, a system error—but the logical pathways were dissolving into a fog of sheer terror. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my living room, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. I kept catching glimpses of the street outside my window, the familiar oak tree seeming menacingly close. I needed air, needed distance, needed anything but this paralyzing certainty that the past was suddenly trying to reclaim its territory.
